questions.q: What is the difference between authentication and authorization?
questions.a: Authentication verifies identity; authorization checks permissions.

questions.q: A JWT consists of three parts. What are they?
Which OAuth grant is used for machine-to-machine communication?
In OpenID Connect, what is the purpose of the ID token?
questions.a: To prove the user has authenticated

questions.q: What makes WebAuthn resistant to phishing?
questions.a: It binds credentials to the real origin and uses public-key cryptography.

questions.q: Why is HS256 less suitable than RS256 for distributed microservices?
questions.a: A shared HMAC secret must be distributed widely, while RSA lets services verify without sharing the signing key.

questions.q: Why are short-lived access tokens paired with refresh tokens?
questions.a: Access tokens limit exposure, while refresh tokens let the client obtain new access tokens safely.

questions.q: Why is SHA-256 unsuitable for password hashing?
questions.a: It is too fast and easy to brute force; password hashing needs deliberate slowness and salting.

questions.q: How does RBAC differ from ABAC?
questions.a: RBAC grants access by role; ABAC evaluates attributes and policy conditions.

questions.q: What problem does rate limiting help with in auth systems?
questions.a: It slows brute-force attacks and reduces abuse of login and token endpoints.

questions.q: Why is a session cookie often simpler than a JWT for browser apps?
questions.a: The server can revoke or rotate session state centrally without shipping token logic to the client.

questions.q: Why is token signing not the same as token encryption?
questions.a: Signing proves integrity and origin, but the payload can still be readable unless it is separately encrypted.

questions.q: Why do password hashing algorithms use cost factors or memory hardness?
questions.a: To make offline attacks expensive and slow down brute-force guessing.

questions.q: What tradeoff do stateless JWTs make compared with sessions?
questions.a: They simplify horizontal scaling, but revocation and immediate invalidation become harder.

questions.q: Why can WebAuthn improve account security even if passwords are reused elsewhere?
questions.a: The private key never leaves the authenticator and the credential is origin-bound.

questions.q: Why does OIDC exist on top of OAuth 2.0?
questions.a: OAuth handles authorization, while OIDC adds a standard identity layer for authentication.

questions.q: Why is auth often described as a system, not a single login endpoint?
questions.a: It spans identity proofing, session handling, token issuance, revocation, authorization, and monitoring.
